title: The Negroï؟½s Complaint.
content:
آ آ آ آ Forced from home and all its pleasures,
آ آ آ آ Africï؟½s coast I left forlorn;
آ آ آ آ To increase a strangerï؟½s treasures,
آ آ آ آ Oï؟½er the raging billows borne.
آ آ آ آ Men from England bought and sold me,
آ آ آ آ Paid my price in paltry gold;
آ آ آ آ But, though slave they have enrollï؟½d me,
آ آ آ آ Minds are never to be sold.


آ آ آ آ Still in thought as free as ever,
آ آ آ آ What are Englandï؟½s rights, I ask,
آ آ آ آ Me from my delights to sever,
آ آ آ آ Me to torture, me to task?
آ آ آ آ Fleecy locks and black complexion
آ آ آ آ Cannot forfeit natureï؟½s claim;
آ آ آ آ Skins may differ, but affection
آ آ آ آ Dwells in white and black the same.


آ آ آ آ Why did all-creating Nature
آ آ آ آ Make the plant for which we toil?
آ آ آ آ Sighs must fan it, tears must water,
آ آ آ آ Sweat of ours must dress the soil.
آ آ آ آ Think, ye masters iron-hearted,
آ آ آ آ Lolling at your jovial boards,
آ آ آ آ Think how many backs have smarted
آ آ آ آ For the sweets your cane affords.


آ آ آ آ Is there, as ye sometimes tells us,
آ آ آ آ Is there One who reigns on high?
آ آ آ آ Has he bid you buy and sell us,
آ آ آ آ Speaking from his throne, the sky?
آ آ آ آ Ask him, if your knotted scourges,
آ آ آ آ Matches, blood-extorting screws,
آ آ آ آ Are the means that duty urges
آ آ آ آ Agents of his will to use?


آ آ آ آ Hark! he answersï؟½wild tornadoes,
آ آ آ آ Strewing yonder sea with wrecks;
آ آ آ آ Wasting towns, plantations, meadows,
آ آ آ آ Are the voice with which he speaks.
آ آ آ آ He, foreseeing what vexations
آ آ آ آ Africï؟½s sons should undergo,
آ آ آ آ Fixï؟½d their tyrantsï؟½ habitations
آ آ آ آ Where his whirlwinds answerï؟½no.


آ آ آ آ By our blood in Afric wasted,
آ آ آ آ Ere our necks received the chain;
آ آ آ آ By the miseries that we tasted,
آ آ آ آ Crossing in your barks the main;
آ آ آ آ By our sufferings, since ye brought us
آ آ آ آ To the man-degrading mart,
آ آ آ آ All sustainï؟½d by patience, taught us
آ آ آ آ Only by a broken heart;


آ آ آ آ Deem our nation brutes no longer,
آ آ آ آ Till some reason ye shall find
آ آ آ آ Worthier of regard, and stronger
آ آ آ آ Than the colour of our kind.
آ آ آ آ Slaves of gold, whose sordid dealings
آ آ آ آ Tarnish all your boasted powers,
آ آ آ آ Prove that you have human feelings,
آ آ آ آ Ere you proudly question ours!
meaning:
This evocative piece by William Cowper represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of William Cowper's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
